food_name: Lu Rou Fan (Taiwanese Braised Pork Rice)
aliases: 爌肉飯 (kuàng ròu fàn, southern Taiwan)

chinese: 爌肉飯 (kuàng ròu fàn, southern Taiwan)

food_subgroup: Rice dish with braised pork — steamed jasmine rice topped with slow-braised minced or diced pork belly in dark soy and five-spice sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for lu rou fan specifically. Closest parent concept: rice bowl dish with braised pork topping (FOODON:03315571 — rice dish, approximate). No exact match available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Steamed jasmine rice with slow-braised minced pork belly in dark soy and five-spice sauce (lu rou fan / 滷肉飯); composite dish, no exact FoodEx2 code available
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for lu rou fan. Closest parent: mixed rice dish with meat topping. Code not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for lu rou fan. Not a clinical substance; no mapping attempted.

serving.description: 1 bowl (~350g: 200g steamed jasmine rice + 150g braised pork belly sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of lu rou fan weighs approximately 350g, comprising 200g of steamed jasmine rice topped with 150g of slow-braised minced or diced pork belly in dark soy and five-spice sauce, delivering approximately 658 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia (Taiwan data); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — cooked white rice (ID 168878) and pork belly braised (ID 168322); composite dish calculation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Lu rou fan is a composite dish. Per-100g values derived by weighted combination: 200g cooked jasmine rice (FAO/INFOODS SEA Taiwan; ~130 kcal/100g) + 150g braised pork belly sauce (USDA SR Legacy pork belly + soy sauce braising liquid adjustment). Sodium elevated by dark soy sauce braising; values cross-checked against published Taiwanese dietary survey data. Composite per-100g values reflect 350g bowl total weight.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed rice dishes — analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lu rou fan. GI estimated at ~62 (medium) based on: white jasmine rice GI ~73 (University of Sydney GI Database, direct measurement); fat and protein content of braised pork belly sauce significantly delays gastric emptying and blunts postprandial glucose response, reducing effective GI by approximately 10–15 units from the rice-only baseline. GL per 350g bowl = (62 × 80.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 49.9, rounded to 50. This is classified VERY HIGH GL (≥20). Estimation methodology consistent with Brand-Miller et al. mixed-meal GI principles.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1,680mg sodium per 350g bowl, primarily from dark soy sauce and light soy sauce concentrated during slow braising
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should treat lu rou fan as an occasional dish (maximum once weekly); request 'less sauce (少醬汁)' to reduce sodium by approximately 300–400mg per bowl, avoid adding soy sauce at the table, and pair with unseasoned steamed vegetables to reduce overall meal sodium density.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load of 50 per bowl driven by 80.5g available carbohydrate from white jasmine rice; GI ~62 (medium) partially mitigated by fat and protein from pork belly sauce
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit lu rou fan to small portions (half bowl, ~175g) or substitute brown rice to reduce GL; the 658 kcal per bowl combined with GL 50 poses significant risk for postprandial hyperglycaemia and weight management in T2DM; monitor blood glucose response and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.8g per bowl from pork belly skin and fat layer) combined with very high sodium (~1,680mg per bowl) and 35mg cholesterol per 100g composite
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit lu rou fan consumption due to the combined burden of saturated fat (promoting LDL elevation), very high sodium (promoting hypertension and fluid retention), and caloric density (658 kcal per bowl); if consumed, request lean pork preparation, less sauce, and accompany with fibre-rich vegetables to partially offset lipid and sodium impact.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat from pork belly promotes arachidonic acid conversion to pro-inflammatory eicosanoids; high sodium may exacerbate autoimmune inflammation via Th17 pathway upregulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consume lu rou fan in moderation; the saturated fat and sodium content may worsen systemic inflammation, and requesting leaner pork preparation with reduced sauce is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lu rou fan (滷肉飯, lǔ ròu fàn) is the quintessential Taiwanese comfort food — a bowl of steamed jasmine rice topped with slow-braised minced or diced pork belly in a deeply umami, gently spiced dark soy and five-spice sauce. A standard serving is approximately 350g (200g steamed rice + 150g braised pork sauce), delivering approximately 658 kcal, 28.7g protein, 26.3g fat, and 80.5g carbohydrate. Sodium is very high at approximately 1,680mg per bowl due to the concentration of dark soy sauce and light soy sauce during prolonged braising. The dish has a GI of approximately 62 (medium) and a glycemic load of 50 per bowl — classified VERY HIGH GL — making it a significant concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or obesity. The pork belly is braised for 1–2 hours with dark soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, rock sugar, star anise, five-spice powder, fried shallots, and garlic until the fat renders and the sauce becomes glossy and caramelised. Northern Taiwanese style uses finely minced or diced pork belly; southern Taiwan (Tainan) uses larger braised pork chunks known as 爌肉 (kuàng ròu). The dish is NOT halal (pork) and is dairy-free. It is ubiquitous across Taiwan at night market stalls, cafeterias, and dedicated lu rou fan restaurants, and is considered a national comfort dish accessible across all economic strata. Key clinical concerns: very high sodium (hypertension risk), very high GL (T2DM and obesity risk), and high saturated fat from pork belly (cardiovascular risk). Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA Taiwan data + USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, composite calculation. Confidence: HIGH.

five-spice: star anise cinnamon clove fennel pepper blend

confidence_reason: Per-100g nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Taiwan food composition data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked white rice and braised pork belly), with composite dish calculation. Values are internally consistent: energy check = (8.2 × 4) + (7.5 × 9) + (23.0 × 4) = 32.8 + 67.5 + 92.0 = 192.3 kcal/100g vs reported 188.0 kcal (within acceptable rounding range for composite dish with sauce water content). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice baseline with documented fat/protein attenuation. Confidence HIGH.
notes: 1. COMPOSITE DISH: Lu rou fan is a composite dish; per-100g values represent the weighted average of the 350g bowl (200g steamed jasmine rice + 150g braised pork belly sauce). Individual component ratios may vary by restaurant or home preparation. 2. SODIUM VARIABILITY: Sodium content is highly variable depending on the amount of braising sauce served; 480mg/100g (1,680mg/bowl) represents a typical restaurant serving. Requesting 'less sauce (少醬汁)' may reduce sodium by 20–25%. 3. GI ESTIMATION: No direct GI measurement exists for lu rou fan as a composite dish. GI ~62 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice GI ~73 (University of Sydney GI Database, direct measurement) attenuated by fat and protein content of braised pork belly sauce per Brand-Miller mixed-meal methodology. 4. WATER CONTENT: water_g set to -1 as precise moisture data for the composite braised dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA Taiwan or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry. 5. MONOUNSATURATED AND POLYUNSATURATED FAT: monounsaturated_fat_g (3.1g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly fatty acid profile proportionally applied to composite fat content; these are estimates. 6. NOT HALAL: Contains pork — not suitable for halal dietary requirements. 7. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for this preparation type per USDA SR Legacy pork belly data; naturally zero for this traditional braised preparation without hydrogenated fats.
